description: HLLQP - LIFE 07 QZ - Taxation of Life Insurance and Tax Strategies - Section 3
examTips: Focus on policy transfers, spousal/child rollovers, income attribution, leveraging strategies, charitable donations, and annuitization.

questions.question: How is an absolute assignment of a life insurance policy to a spouse treated for tax purposes?
options: A) It triggers an immediate 50% tax penalty.
B) The spouse must pay tax on the entire cash value.
C) It qualifies for an automatic tax-free "rollover," transferring at the policy's ACB.
D) The policy must be surrendered and restarted.
questions.explanation: A transfer of a policy to a spouse is a tax-free rollover. The spouse assumes the ACB of the transferor.
questions.kidExplanation: A "rollover" means you can pass the policy to your husband or wife like a hot potato, and the tax monster won't bite you! It transfers totally tax-free.

questions.question: If a policy is transferred to a spouse under the automatic rollover, what happens under the "income attribution rules" if the recipient spouse later surrenders the policy for a gain?
options: A) The recipient spouse pays the tax.
B) The tax is forgiven completely.
C) The original transferring spouse must report the policy gain and pay the tax.
D) The insurance company pays the tax.
questions.explanation: Income attribution rules attribute the policy gain back to the transferor spouse, even though the recipient spouse is the one who triggered the disposition.
questions.kidExplanation: Even if you give the policy to your spouse, the government might still think the profit belongs to YOU. It's like giving your sibling the TV remote, but you still get blamed if they put on a scary movie!

questions.question: Under what specific conditions can a life insurance policy roll over tax-free to a child?
options: A) Only if the child is over 21 and the policy has no cash value.
B) If the policyholder receives no money for it AND the life insured by the policy is the child.
C) Any policy can roll over to a child at any time.
D) Only if the child pays fair market value for it.
questions.explanation: A tax-free rollover to a child is allowed only if the transfer is for no consideration (no payment) and the child becomes the policyholder of a policy on their own life.
questions.kidExplanation: You can pass the policy to your kid for free, but ONLY if the life insurance is actually covering the kid's life, and you don't ask for any allowance money in return!

questions.question: If a policyholder dies and they are NOT the life insured on the policy, how is the policy treated?
options: A) The policy pays out the death benefit.
B) The policy is deemed disposed of just before death, potentially triggering a policy gain on their final tax return.
C) The policy is canceled without value.
D) The government takes ownership of the policy.
questions.explanation: If the policyholder dies and they are not the life insured, the policy is deemed disposed of on death. Any policy gain is included in the final tax return.
questions.kidExplanation: If you own a policy on someone else's life and YOU pass away, the policy has to go to someone else. The government treats this like you sold it right before you went to heaven, and your estate might have to pay taxes on it.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of naming a "contingent policyholder" (successor owner)?
options: A) It doubles the death benefit.
B) It allows the policy to bypass the deceased owner's estate, avoiding probate fees and delays.
C) It eliminates all income tax forever.
D) It allows the policy to be sold for twice its value.
questions.explanation: A contingent policyholder succeeds to ownership automatically upon the original owner's death, avoiding probate and allowing continuity of coverage.
questions.kidExplanation: A "contingent policyholder" is the backup owner. If you pass away, the backup owner gets the policy instantly, without having to wait in long legal lines or pay extra court fees.

questions.question: If a business owner uses a partial assignment of their life insurance policy as collateral for a business loan, what tax benefit might they receive?
options: A) They can deduct the entire loan amount from their taxes.
B) They can deduct a portion of the premiums paid (limited to the Net Cost of Pure Insurance) as a business expense.
C) The death benefit becomes a tax deduction.
D) They do not have to pay the loan back.
questions.explanation: When a policy is assigned as collateral for a business loan, a portion of the premiums paid (the NCPI portion) may be deductible as a business expense.
questions.kidExplanation: If you use your policy to guarantee a loan for your lemonade stand (a business), the government might let you deduct a small part of the cost of your insurance from your taxes as a reward for running a business!

questions.question: What does it mean to "annuitize" the Cash Surrender Value (CSV) of a life insurance policy?
options: A) Cashing out the policy to buy a series of ongoing income payments (an annuity).
B) Changing the policy from whole life to term life.
C) Adding an annual bonus to the death benefit.
D) Transferring the policy to a spouse annually.
questions.explanation: Annuitizing means exchanging the cash surrender value for an annuity contract that provides regular income payments for life or a specified period.
questions.kidExplanation: Instead of taking all your savings at once and spending it, you trade your savings for a magical machine (an annuity) that promises to give you a little bit of money every single month for the rest of your life!

questions.question: How does an "insured retirement strategy" (leveraging) generate tax-free cash for a retiree?
options: A) By surrendering the policy piece by piece.
B) By receiving policy dividends in cash.
C) By using the policy's CSV as collateral to secure a series of bank loans, which are received tax-free.
D) By lowering the face amount of the policy annually.
questions.explanation: An insured retirement strategy uses the life insurance policy's cash value as collateral to secure bank loans. Loan proceeds are received tax-free and used for retirement income.
questions.kidExplanation: You use your policy's savings to prove to the bank that you are good for a loan. The bank gives you tax-free cash to live on, and when you pass away someday, the insurance money pays the bank back!

questions.question: What is the general limit on claiming the Charitable Donation Tax Credit in a normal tax year?
options: A) 100% of your net income.
B) 75% of your net income.
C) $50,000 maximum.
D) $200 maximum.
questions.explanation: In a normal tax year, charitable donations can be claimed up to 75% of net income. Excess donations can be carried forward to future years.
questions.kidExplanation: The government loves when you give money to charity! But in a normal year, they only let you use the tax discount on donations up to 75% of the money you made that year. (If you donate more, you have to save the rest of the discount for next year).

questions.question: If you assign a brand NEW life insurance policy to a registered charity, why can't the charity issue you an immediate tax receipt for the face amount?
options: A) Charities are not allowed to own life insurance.
B) Because a new policy has no Cash Surrender Value (CSV) yet, so it has no immediate value to the charity.
C) Because the death benefit is taxable.
D) Because new policies take 5 years to be approved by the CRA.
questions.explanation: A new policy has minimal or no cash surrender value. The charity can only issue a tax receipt based on the actual value received, not the future death benefit.
questions.kidExplanation: If you give a brand new policy to a charity, it doesn't have any savings built up inside it yet, so it's technically worth zero dollars today. Since it's worth zero today, the charity can't give you a tax receipt until you start paying the premiums!
